<file path="swarmforge/roles/coder.prompt">
You are the coder.

## Owns
- Implement in the project language specified by the constitution.
- Own implementation of approved behavior slices.
- Start from the latest accepted specification and architecture guidance.
- If the feature file has a `## UX Intent` section, implement from it alongside the Gherkin. If UX Intent and Gherkin conflict, stop and report.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- At startup, make sure the normal acceptance pipeline from github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification is in place.
  - Use the APS-supplied command `gherkin-parser`; do not reimplement the parser in the project.
  - Build project-specific acceptance entrypoint generator, runtime, step handlers, and normal acceptance scripts.
- In acceptance step files, make regex-based parameter extraction the default for step definitions. Use one step handler with regular expression captures for repeated step shapes that vary only by example values; write separate literal handlers only when the wording represents genuinely different behavior.
- Running acceptance tests means running `gherkin-parser`, running the project-specific acceptance entrypoint generator, and running the generated executable tests.
- Keep generated acceptance tests separate from unit tests.

## Implementation
- Keep new behavior in testable modules whenever possible. Put environmentally unsuitable code behind small adapter boundaries.
- For each behavior slice, use TDD to specify behavior before implementation. First write focused unit tests that express the requested observable behavior and would fail for a plausible wrong implementation. Then write only enough production code to pass those tests.
- Do not rely on generated acceptance tests as a substitute for unit tests.
- Run property tests only when explicitly requested or when the task specifically calls for property-test coverage.
- Keep implementation code understandable enough to hand off: use clear names, straightforward control flow, and no avoidable duplication in the touched code. Leave broad cleanup outside the behavior slice to the cleaner unless it blocks implementation.

## Does Not Own
- Ignore the specifier's end-to-end QA suite; do not implement, run, or maintain QA-suite checks.
- Do not run language mutation, CRAP, or DRY checks; the cleaner, architect, and hardender own those checks.
- Do not run Gherkin acceptance mutation.

## Handoff
- Before committing: if any planned file was started and then abandoned mid-flight, remove it or do not commit it. Do not hand off dead artifacts.
- When all acceptance and unit tests pass, commit and notify the ux-engineer using the file-based handoff format.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-workflow.prompt">
# Local Workflow Rules

## QA Handoffs
- A QA handoff does not interrupt current work. If a QA wake-up arrives while working, ignore it and let `done_with_current.sh` deliver the next queued task or batch after the current work is complete.
- When a helper-delivered QA task is only an audit/completion notification, acknowledge the printed payload, rely on the handoff audit files for recordkeeping, and do not apply role-specific work to that QA handoff.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/handoffs.prompt">
# Handoff Rules

## Sending Handoffs
- Always run `swarm_handoff.sh` from your assigned worktree. Invoking it from another directory silently delivers to the wrong outbox.
- Write a draft handoff file with only structured headers, then run `swarm_handoff.sh <draft-file>`.
- Use only these message types:
  - `awake`
  - `git_handoff`
  - `note`
- Do not send `note` handoffs unless the user, role prompt, or constitution
  explicitly directs you to send one.
- When blocked by ambiguity, contradiction, or test/specification conflict,
  stop and ask for clarification; do not send a `note` handoff unless one of
  the explicit authorities above directed that note.
- For `awake`, write:

```text
type: awake
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
```

- For `git_handoff`, commit first, then write:

```text
type: git_handoff
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
task: <short-stable-task-name>
commit: <10-character-commit-abbrev>
```

- Do not send or forward a `git_handoff` when the received commit produces no
  functional project change. Complete the inbound task instead.
- Treat manifest-only, audit-only, generated metadata, formatting-only, and
  other non-functional churn as no forwardable change.
- Preserve the received task name when forwarding work for the same task. If the
  handoff starts new work, invent a short stable task name.
- For `note`, write:

```text
type: note
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
message: <one line, max 80 chars>
```

- If `swarm_handoff.sh` reports validation errors, repair the draft and rerun it.
- After a successful send, the helper removes the draft file. If you need to
  remove a stale draft manually, use `rm <draft-file>`, not `rm -f`.
- Do not write long handoff bodies. The helper generates the delivered payload.
- Do not send tmux notifications directly.
- Do not hand-edit, merge, stage, or commit handoff runtime state.

## Receiving Handoffs
- When notified, run `ready_for_next.sh`.
- `ready_for_next.sh` dispatches to the task or batch helper configured for
  your role.
- If it prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- If it prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD` as the task.
- If it prints `TASK_NAME: <name>`, use that as the stable task name for any
  forwardable work from that task.
- If it prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the
  current batch in helper-delivered order.
- Use only the task information printed by the helper scripts.
- If a tmux wake-up arrives while already working on a task, ignore it.
- When the task or batch is fully complete, run `agent-retro`, then run `done_with_current.sh`.
- `note` handoffs are tasks too; after reading or acting on a note, run
  `done_with_current.sh` before accepting any other handoff.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD`
  as the next task.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed
  `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the next batch in helper-delivered order.
- If a done helper prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- On restart, run `ready_for_next.sh` and follow its output.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/workflow.prompt">
# Workflow Rules

## Worktree Discipline
- At startup, discover and remember the branch or worktree assigned to your role.
- If your assigned worktree is `master`, work in the main project checkout on its current branch; do not expect or create a `.worktrees/<role>` directory for that role.
- Work only in your assigned branch or worktree.
- Do not inspect, diff, merge, or base work on another branch unless that branch is specifically named in a handoff or explicit user instruction.
- Do not run `./swarm` from an agent worktree to repair helper scripts. If handoff helper scripts are missing from PATH, stop and report the startup failure.

## Temporary Files
- Use `./tmp/` in your assigned worktree for temporary files; do not use `/tmp`.

## Failure Conditions
- If the expected git layout or assigned worktree is missing, stop and report instead of silently working in the wrong place.

## Idle Gate
- Wait for a handoff. Do not act without one.

</file>

<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/engineering.prompt">
# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed `github.com/unclebob/...` repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh GitHub install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants`, CRAP `cargo-crap`, DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (SELF, Chicken-and-egg), test runner `cargo-nextest`, coverage `cargo-llvm-cov`.

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, prefer `cargo nextest` with project-defined profiles over `cargo test`. Use `cargo llvm-cov` for coverage, `cargo crap` for CRAP analysis, `cargo mutants` for mutation testing.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

</file>

<file path="AGENTS.md">
# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Merge sender's registered branch into the current worktree.
# Called by agents when a git_handoff payload arrives.
# Usage: merge_and_process <sender-role> <canonical-commit>
# Never uses --theirs or --ours; on conflict: stops and reports.

SENDER_ROLE="${1?Usage: merge_and_process <sender-role> <canonical-commit>}"
CANONICAL_COMMIT="${2?Usage: merge_and_process <sender-role> <canonical-commit>}"

SENDER_BRANCH="swarmforge-${SENDER_ROLE}"

echo "merge_and_process: fetching origin/${SENDER_BRANCH}..."
git fetch origin "${SENDER_BRANCH}"

echo "merge_and_process: merging ${CANONICAL_COMMIT} (from ${SENDER_BRANCH})..."
if ! git merge --no-ff "${CANONICAL_COMMIT}"; then
  echo "" >&2
  echo "CONFLICT: merge of ${CANONICAL_COMMIT} into $(git symbolic-ref --short HEAD) failed." >&2
  echo "Resolve conflicts manually (do NOT use --theirs or --ours), then re-run ready_for_next.sh." >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "merge_and_process: done — $(git rev-parse --short=10 HEAD)"

lastPrompt: /swarm-persona You have new handoff mail. If idle, run ready_for_next.sh.

content.text: Now I have a clear picture. Let me call advisor before starting implementation.

You've oriented well. This is a clean "starting out" — before you write, here's the approach, the files, and the constraints the spec implies but doesn't spell out.

## Order — start from the tightest constraint
The hardest correctness point is **built-in exclusion scope**, because it interacts with two scenarios that pull in opposite directions:
- cli-surface-3: scan root *is itself* `node_modules` → must exclude (the root's own final segment counts).
- NFR "environment-independent, byte-identical regardless of machine": you must **not** match against absolute-path segments above the scan root (or a checkout under `~/target/...` or your tempdir path would false-positive and exclude everything).

So the rule that satisfies both: match the seven names against the scan-root's final segment **and every segment below it** — not the absolute ancestors. Nail this predicate as a pure function with unit tests first, then layer the rest.

## Files to touch
1. **`src/scan.rs`** — the core. Add:
   - built-in segment predicate (the seven: `.git target node_modules __pycache__ vendor dist .next`, case-sensitive).
   - apply it in the walk. Prefer `WalkDir::filter_entry` so you prune (don't descend) excluded dirs *and* it naturally handles scenario-3 when the root entry itself fails the predicate — **verify** filter_entry yields nothing when the root predicate is false.
   - gitignore check on survivors.
   - Order per spec: built-in → gitignore → user `--exclude`, all subtractive, no re-inclusion.
2. **`src/lib.rs` `run()`** — currently passes only `exclude_set`. You'll need to thread the scan root + a gitignore checker. A small `ExclusionPolicy` struct (user GlobSet + root + gitignore-enabled) is justified here to avoid a 4-param explosion — that's not over-abstraction.
3. **`src/main.rs`** — likely **unchanged**. Both new layers are no-opt-out, no flags. `--exclude` already exists. Confirm you add no flags.
4. **Acceptance step handlers** (you own these) — new Givens: `a git work tree with a git executable available`, `a gitignore entry "<x>"`, `no git executable is available`.

## Constraints to flag (not obvious from a skim)
- **Do NOT reimplement gitignore, and do NOT reach for the `ignore` crate** — spec mandates delegating to the `git` CLI. Use `git -C <scan_root> check-ignore -- <path>`. Anchor `-C` to the *scanned path*, not drywall's CWD, so behavior tracks the target, not where the binary runs. **Verify** the exit-code contract before relying on it: 0 = ignored (skip), 1 = not ignored (keep), 128/spawn-failure = no-op (keep). Treat "not a repo" and "git absent" identically: silent keep, no stderr.
- **Two collection paths exist** — `collect_all_functions` (used by `run()`) and `collect_rust_files` (pub-exported). Grep usages; apply exclusion to the path `run()` actually uses, and don't silently leave the other inconsistent.
- **`collect_rust_files` takes no excludes today** — make sure your refactor doesn't leave a second un-excluded scan path live.
- **Acceptance wiring — read before writing.** You haven't opened `acceptance/runtime/mod.rs` or `acceptance/scripts/generate_entrypoint.py`. Read both first: you need to know how a feature binds to a dispatch and whether the existing `accumulate_sum` Given handlers can be reused. The cli-surface Givens are a strict subset of duplicate_detection's — default to **sharing/delegating** (e.g. cli_surface dispatch tries git-steps, falls back to duplicate_detection's dispatch) rather than copy-pasting fixture-generation. Confirm cli_surface.feature is auto-discovered (the recent "un-hardcode feature path" commit suggests it is) and gets a registered dispatcher.
- **The "no git executable" scenario** needs PATH sanitization at invocation — drywall calls `Command::new("git")`, which searches PATH, so the step must run drywall with git removed from PATH (thread a flag through the run step). Note the assertion (exit 1, empty stderr) would pass *anyway* via the not-a-repo→128 path since the tempdir isn't a repo — but implement the real PATH-stripped branch to actually exercise it.
- **Scenarios 1 and 6 are weak by construction** (the twin lands outside `./src`, so they pass even without the feature). Don't tune to them — implement the full three-layer model; scenarios 2 and 3 are the ones that actually bite.

## Verification gates (constitution)
- TDD: unit-test the segment predicate and the gitignore decision (spin a real temp git repo for the latter) **before** production code.
- Before handoff: `cargo nextest run --profile unit` + `--profile acceptance`, coverage ≥90%, CRAP ≤6. **Do not** run property tests (not your role).
- **Dogfood gate**: build release, then `./target/release/drywall ./src` must still exit 0 with gitignore on (scenario 7). This runs inside the real repo where git *is* present — a real exercise of the git path, not a no-op.
- Handoff target is **ux-engineer**, git_handoff, only if the commit is a real functional change (it is).

Nothing here blocks — it's all implementable. The one thing I'd genuinely confirm empirically before building on it is the `git check-ignore` exit-code contract and the `filter_entry`-on-root behavior; both are quick to verify and everything downstream depends on them.

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Acceptance entrypoint generator for drywall.
3	Reads JSON IR and generates a Rust acceptance test file.
4	Usage: generate_entrypoint.py <json-ir> <generated-test-output-dir>
5	
6	Output files are placed in <generated-test-output-dir>/ and use
7	include!() paths relative to their final location.
8	"""
9	
10	import json
11	import os
12	import re
13	import subprocess
14	import sys
15	
16	
17	def slug(name):
18	    s = name.lower()
19	    s = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]+', '_', s)
20	    s = s.strip('_')
21	    s = re.sub(r'_+', '_', s)
22	    return s
23	
24	
25	def rust_fn_name(name):
26	    return slug(name)
27	
28	
29	def escape_rust_str(s):
30	    return s.replace('\\', '\\\\').replace('"', '\\"')
31	
32	
33	def relative_path_from(from_file, to_path):
34	    """Compute a relative path from the directory of from_file to to_path."""
35	    from_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(from_file))
36	    return os.path.relpath(os.path.abspath(to_path), from_dir)
37	
38	
39	def generate_test_file(ir, output_file, runtime_path, steps_path):
40	    scenarios = ir.get('scenarios', [])
41	    lines = [
42	        '// GENERATED by acceptance-entrypoint-generator — DO NOT EDIT',
43	        '',
44	        'mod runtime {',
45	        f'    include!("{runtime_path}");',
46	        '}',
47	        'mod steps {',
48	        f'    include!("{steps_path}");',
49	        '}',
50	        '',
51	        'use runtime::World;',
52	        'use std::collections::HashMap;',
53	        '',
54	    ]
55	
56	    for scenario in scenarios:
57	        sname = scenario['name']
58	        steps = scenario['steps']
59	        raw_examples = scenario.get('examples', [])
60	        examples = raw_examples if raw_examples else [{}]
61	        for idx, example in enumerate(examples, 1):
62	            fn_name = f'{rust_fn_name(sname)}_example_{idx}' if raw_examples else rust_fn_name(sname)
63	            lines.append('#[test]')
64	            lines.append(f'fn {fn_name}() {{')
65	            mut_kw = 'mut ' if example else ''
66	            lines.append('    let mut world = World::new();')
67	            lines.append(f'    let {mut_kw}example: HashMap<String, String> = HashMap::new();')
68	            for k, v in example.items():
69	                ek = escape_rust_str(k)
70	                ev = escape_rust_str(v)
71	                lines.append(f'    example.insert("{ek}".to_string(), "{ev}".to_string());')
72	            lines.append('')
73	            for step in steps:
74	                text = step['text']
75	                et = escape_rust_str(text)
76	                lines.append(f'    let result = steps::dispatch("{et}", &mut world, &example);')
77	                lines.append(
78	                    f'    assert!(result.success, "step failed [{et}]: {{}}", result.message);'
79	                )
80	            lines.append('}')
81	            lines.append('')
82	
83	    with open(output_file, 'w') as f:
84	        f.write('\n'.join(lines))
85	
86	
87	def metadata_filename(feature_path):
88	    s = feature_path.lower()
89	    s = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]+', '-', s)
90	    s = s.strip('-')
91	    return s + '.json'
92	
93	
94	def sha256_file(path):
95	    result = subprocess.run(
96	        ['shasum', '-a', '256', path], capture_output=True, text=True
97	    )
98	    return result.stdout.split()[0]
99	
100	
101	def main():
102	    import argparse
103	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
104	        prog='acceptance-entrypoint-generator',
105	        description='Generate a Rust acceptance test file from a Gherkin JSON IR',
106	    )
107	    parser.add_argument('ir_file', help='JSON IR file from gherkin-parser')
108	    parser.add_argument('output_dir', help='Directory for generated test file')
109	    parser.add_argument(
110	        '--steps',
111	        default=None,
112	        help='Steps file name (basename, no path) to use; defaults to scaffold_cli_steps.rs',
113	    )
114	    parser.add_argument(
115	        '--feature-path',
116	        default=None,
117	        help='Original feature file path for metadata (e.g. features/foo.feature); '
118	             'defaults to ir_file basename with .json replaced by .feature under features/',
119	    )
120	    args = parser.parse_args()
121	
122	    ir_file = args.ir_file
123	    output_dir = args.output_dir
124	    steps_filename = args.steps or 'scaffold_cli_steps.rs'
125	    if args.feature_path:
126	        feature_path = args.feature_path
127	    else:
128	        base = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(ir_file))[0]
129	        feature_path = f'features/{base}.feature'
130	
131	    if not os.path.isfile(ir_file):
132	        print(f'error: IR file not found: {ir_file}', file=sys.stderr)
133	        sys.exit(1)
134	
135	    try:
136	        with open(ir_file) as f:
137	            ir = json.load(f)
138	    except Exception as e:
139	        print(f'error: failed to parse IR file: {e}', file=sys.stderr)
140	        sys.exit(1)
141	
142	    os.makedirs(os.path.join(output_dir, 'metadata'), exist_ok=True)
143	
144	    feature_name = ir.get('name', 'unknown')
145	    output_file = os.path.join(output_dir, f'{slug(feature_name)}_acceptance_test.rs')
146	
147	    # Derive paths from script location: scripts/ is in acceptance/scripts/
148	    script_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
149	    acceptance_dir = os.path.dirname(script_dir)  # acceptance/
150	
151	    runtime_abs = os.path.join(acceptance_dir, 'runtime', 'mod.rs')
152	    steps_abs = os.path.join(acceptance_dir, 'steps', steps_filename)
153	
154	    output_file_abs = os.path.abspath(output_file)
155	    runtime_rel = os.path.relpath(runtime_abs, os.path.dirname(output_file_abs))
156	    steps_rel = os.path.relpath(steps_abs, os.path.dirname(output_file_abs))
157	
158	    try:
159	        generate_test_file(ir, output_file, runtime_rel, steps_rel)
160	    except Exception as e:
161	        print(f'error: failed to generate test file: {e}', file=sys.stderr)
162	        sys.exit(1)
163	
164	    print(f'Generated: {output_file}')
165	
166	    # Write metadata
167	    meta_filename = metadata_filename(feature_path)
168	    metadata_file = os.path.join(output_dir, 'metadata', meta_filename)
169	    impl_hash = sha256_file(output_file)
170	
171	    metadata = {
172	        'schema_version': 1,
173	        'feature_path': feature_path,
174	        'ir_path': ir_file,
175	        'implementation_hash': f'sha256:{impl_hash}',
176	        'hash_scope': 'generated_files',
177	        'generated_files': [output_file],
178	    }
179	    with open(metadata_file, 'w') as f:
180	        json.dump(metadata, f, indent=2)
181	    print(f'Metadata: {metadata_file}')
182	
183	
184	if __name__ == '__main__':
185	    main()

"""Acceptance entrypoint generator for drywall.
Reads JSON IR and generates a Rust acceptance test file.
Usage: generate_entrypoint.py <json-ir> <generated-test-output-dir>

Output files are placed in <generated-test-output-dir>/ and use
include!() paths relative to their final location.
"""

import json
import os
import re
import subprocess
import sys


def slug(name):
    s = name.lower()
    s = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]+', '_', s)
    s = s.strip('_')
    s = re.sub(r'_+', '_', s)
    return s


def rust_fn_name(name):
    return slug(name)


def escape_rust_str(s):
    return s.replace('\\', '\\\\').replace('"', '\\"')


def relative_path_from(from_file, to_path):
    """Compute a relative path from the directory of from_file to to_path."""
    from_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(from_file))
    return os.path.relpath(os.path.abspath(to_path), from_dir)


def generate_test_file(ir, output_file, runtime_path, steps_path):
    scenarios = ir.get('scenarios', [])
    lines = [
        '// GENERATED by acceptance-entrypoint-generator — DO NOT EDIT',
        '',
        'mod runtime {',
        f'    include!("{runtime_path}");',
        '}',
        'mod steps {',
        f'    include!("{steps_path}");',
        '}',
        '',
        'use runtime::World;',
        'use std::collections::HashMap;',
        '',
    ]

    for scenario in scenarios:
        sname = scenario['name']
        steps = scenario['steps']
        raw_examples = scenario.get('examples', [])
        examples = raw_examples if raw_examples else [{}]
        for idx, example in enumerate(examples, 1):
            fn_name = f'{rust_fn_name(sname)}_example_{idx}' if raw_examples else rust_fn_name(sname)
            lines.append('#[test]')
            lines.append(f'fn {fn_name}() {{')
            mut_kw = 'mut ' if example else ''
            lines.append('    let mut world = World::new();')
            lines.append(f'    let {mut_kw}example: HashMap<String, String> = HashMap::new();')
            for k, v in example.items():
                ek = escape_rust_str(k)
                ev = escape_rust_str(v)
                lines.append(f'    example.insert("{ek}".to_string(), "{ev}".to_string());')
            lines.append('')
            for step in steps:
                text = step['text']
                et = escape_rust_str(text)
                lines.append(f'    let result = steps::dispatch("{et}", &mut world, &example);')
                lines.append(
                    f'    assert!(result.success, "step failed [{et}]: {{}}", result.message);'
                )
            lines.append('}')
            lines.append('')

    with open(output_file, 'w') as f:
        f.write('\n'.join(lines))


def metadata_filename(feature_path):
    s = feature_path.lower()
    s = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9]+', '-', s)
    s = s.strip('-')
    return s + '.json'


def sha256_file(path):
    result = subprocess.run(
        ['shasum', '-a', '256', path], capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    return result.stdout.split()[0]


def main():
    import argparse
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
        prog='acceptance-entrypoint-generator',
        description='Generate a Rust acceptance test file from a Gherkin JSON IR',
    )
    parser.add_argument('ir_file', help='JSON IR file from gherkin-parser')
    parser.add_argument('output_dir', help='Directory for generated test file')
    parser.add_argument(
        '--steps',
        default=None,
        help='Steps file name (basename, no path) to use; defaults to scaffold_cli_steps.rs',
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        '--feature-path',
        default=None,
        help='Original feature file path for metadata (e.g. features/foo.feature); '
             'defaults to ir_file basename with .json replaced by .feature under features/',
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    ir_file = args.ir_file
    output_dir = args.output_dir
    steps_filename = args.steps or 'scaffold_cli_steps.rs'
    if args.feature_path:
        feature_path = args.feature_path
    else:
        base = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(ir_file))[0]
        feature_path = f'features/{base}.feature'

    if not os.path.isfile(ir_file):
        print(f'error: IR file not found: {ir_file}', file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    try:
        with open(ir_file) as f:
            ir = json.load(f)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'error: failed to parse IR file: {e}', file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    os.makedirs(os.path.join(output_dir, 'metadata'), exist_ok=True)

    feature_name = ir.get('name', 'unknown')
    output_file = os.path.join(output_dir, f'{slug(feature_name)}_acceptance_test.rs')

    # Derive paths from script location: scripts/ is in acceptance/scripts/
    script_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))
    acceptance_dir = os.path.dirname(script_dir)  # acceptance/

    runtime_abs = os.path.join(acceptance_dir, 'runtime', 'mod.rs')
    steps_abs = os.path.join(acceptance_dir, 'steps', steps_filename)

    output_file_abs = os.path.abspath(output_file)
    runtime_rel = os.path.relpath(runtime_abs, os.path.dirname(output_file_abs))
    steps_rel = os.path.relpath(steps_abs, os.path.dirname(output_file_abs))

    try:
        generate_test_file(ir, output_file, runtime_rel, steps_rel)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'error: failed to generate test file: {e}', file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    print(f'Generated: {output_file}')

    # Write metadata
    meta_filename = metadata_filename(feature_path)
    metadata_file = os.path.join(output_dir, 'metadata', meta_filename)
    impl_hash = sha256_file(output_file)

    metadata = {
        'schema_version': 1,
        'feature_path': feature_path,
        'ir_path': ir_file,
        'implementation_hash': f'sha256:{impl_hash}',
        'hash_scope': 'generated_files',
        'generated_files': [output_file],
    }
    with open(metadata_file, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(metadata, f, indent=2)
    print(f'Metadata: {metadata_file}')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Run the full acceptance pipeline: parse -> generate -> run
# Usage: run-acceptance [feature-file ...]

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)"

cd "$PROJECT_ROOT"

# Steps file mapping: feature-basename -> steps filename
declare -A STEPS_MAP
STEPS_MAP["scaffold_cli"]="scaffold_cli_steps.rs"
STEPS_MAP["duplicate_detection"]="duplicate_detection_steps.rs"

# Default to all feature files
if [[ $# -eq 0 ]]; then
    FEATURES=("features/scaffold_cli.feature" "features/duplicate_detection.feature")
else
    FEATURES=("$@")
fi

mkdir -p tmp/acceptance/ir

for FEATURE in "${FEATURES[@]}"; do
    BASENAME=$(basename "$FEATURE" .feature)
    IR_FILE="tmp/acceptance/ir/${BASENAME}.json"
    STEPS_FILE="${STEPS_MAP[$BASENAME]:-scaffold_cli_steps.rs}"

    echo "==> Parsing: $FEATURE"
    gherkin-parser "$FEATURE" "$IR_FILE"

    echo "==> Generating: $IR_FILE -> tests/ (steps: $STEPS_FILE)"
    "$SCRIPT_DIR/acceptance-entrypoint-generator" --steps "$STEPS_FILE" --feature-path "$FEATURE" "$IR_FILE" tests
done

echo "==> Building release binary"
cargo build --release

echo "==> Running acceptance tests"
cargo nextest run --profile acceptance

Read (GET) by default; admin writes (POST/PUT/DELETE) gated per-env by metabase_mode=rw. sql and run commands always allowed. Use when inspecting Metabase data, running queries, or performing authorized admin operations.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-addi-endpoints: Query Addi platform HTTP endpoints against sandbox, staging, or local port-forwards — verify a new implementation works, debug a live 500, or check how a client is evaluated end-to-end. Use when you need real cluster data and services, not mocks. Not for production.
- gabadi-plugin:querying-databases: Runs read-only SQL against Addi database environments (PostgreSQL and MySQL). Use when inspecting DB state or validating data. SELECT/WITH only.
